---
title: "Executable notebook demo"
subtitle: "Quarto + Python — the replacement for myst_nb"
jupyter: python3
execute:
  enabled: true
  echo: true
  cache: true
---

This page demonstrates **Quarto's native Jupyter integration** — the feature
that motivated evaluating Quarto as a successor to the current
`sphinx-book-theme` + `myst_nb` stack.

When this page is rendered, every Python code cell below is executed by a
real Jupyter kernel; outputs (numbers, tables, plots) are captured and
embedded in the HTML. Results are cached via `execute: cache: true`, so
subsequent builds are fast unless the code changes.

## A tiny time-series example

Yuneta is heavy on time-series data. Here's a trivial example generating and
plotting a synthetic signal — the kind of snippet we'd want to embed in
architecture docs to illustrate concepts without shipping a separate
notebook file.

```{python}
#| label: fig-signal
#| fig-cap: "A synthetic time-series (sine + noise), rendered at build time."

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
t = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 500)
signal = np.sin(t) + 0.2 * rng.standard_normal(t.shape)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.plot(t, signal, linewidth=1)
ax.set_xlabel("time")
ax.set_ylabel("value")
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The cell above is a proper Python cell — you can reference the figure with
`@fig-signal`, and the caption is stored as document metadata.

## What this replaces

| Sphinx stack feature | Quarto equivalent |
|---|---|
| `myst_nb` execution of `.md`/`.ipynb` | Native `.qmd` + `jupyter:` metadata |
| `nb_execution_mode = "cache"` | `execute: cache: true` (page-level) |
| `sphinx_thebe` live execution in browser | *(no direct equivalent — Quarto uses OJS / Shiny for interactivity)* |
| `matplotlib` figure embedding | Native, with `fig-cap` / `label` metadata |

::: {.callout-tip}
To build this page locally you need:

```bash
pip install jupyter matplotlib numpy
cd docs/doc.yuneta.io-quarto
quarto render
```

If Quarto isn't installed yet, grab it from <https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/>.
:::
